characters.relation: Partner, son-in-law, then enemy
characters.blurb: Shared the three-headed pact with Caesar and Crassus, married Julia, then fought Caesar’s civil war to death in Egypt.

characters.relation: Protégé who joined the murder
characters.blurb: Favorite whom Caesar spared and advanced; led the Ides conspiracy with Cassius and fell at Philippi.

characters.relation: Stoic enemy of his rise
characters.blurb: Unyielding optimate who fought Caesar’s laws, fled after Pharsalus, and killed himself at Utica rather than take pardon.

characters.relation: Orator; uneasy ally and critic
characters.blurb: Consulted, flattered, and disappointed; his consular prestige and letters frame Caesar’s civilian wars.

characters.relation: Uncle by marriage; populares model
characters.blurb: Seven-times consul whose memory Caesar revived against Sulla’s party—the inheritance of a populares name.

characters.relation: Lieutenant; later triumvir
characters.blurb: Master of the Horse and loyal civil-war captain; gave Caesar’s funeral speech and inherited his popular mantle.

characters.relation: Dictator who nearly proscribed him
characters.blurb: Spared young Caesar reluctantly—“in that young man I see many Mariuses”—after the refusal to divorce Cornelia.

characters.relation: Rich partner of the first compact
characters.blurb: Bankrolled debts and politics until Carrhae killed him and broke the three-way balance with Pompey.

characters.relation: Chief author of the Ides
characters.blurb: Lean, hungry conspirator who drew Brutus in; Caesar’s mistrust of his pale look is Plutarch’s omen.

characters.relation: Demagogue of the Bona Dea scandal
characters.blurb: Tried (they said) to reach Pompeia at the women’s rite; Caesar divorced her—“Caesar’s wife must be above suspicion.”

characters.relation: Daughter; bond to Pompey
characters.blurb: Married to Pompey to seal the alliance; her death in childbirth loosened the last personal tie between the dynasts.

characters.relation: Wife divorced after Bona Dea
characters.blurb: Second wife, dismissed though perhaps innocent, when Clodius’s intrusion stained her household.

characters.relation: Last wife; dreamed the Ides
characters.blurb: Warned Caesar not to go to the Senate; her fears and the omens fill the night before the murder.

characters.relation: Egyptian queen; ally and lover
characters.blurb: Smuggled into Caesar’s quarters at Alexandria; her cause and Caesarion tied Rome’s civil war to the Nile.

characters.relation: Eunuch minister at Alexandria
characters.blurb: Guided young Ptolemy against Cleopatra and insulted Caesar; executed when the palace war turned.

characters.relation: Egyptian general of the siege
characters.blurb: Commanded the forces that trapped Caesar in Alexandria after Pompey’s murder.

characters.relation: Advisor who urged Pompey’s murder
characters.blurb: Rhetor who argued that killing Pompey would please Caesar—and so stained Egypt’s welcome.

characters.relation: Optimate commander in Africa
characters.blurb: Metellus Scipio, Cato’s ally, who held Africa against Caesar and fell after Thapsus.

characters.relation: Name in the optimate Fasti
characters.blurb: Several Metelli oppose Caesar; the Life’s “Metellus” often marks that senatorial bloc beside Cato.

characters.relation: Consular enemy in the civil war
characters.blurb: Named among the Pompeian consulars who fled Rome and stiffened the Senate’s war party.

characters.relation: Held Corfinium; later at Pharsalus
characters.blurb: Domitius Ahenobarbus, early Pompeian opponent Caesar pardoned—then faced again in the Greek campaign.

characters.relation: Mother
characters.blurb: Watched over Caesar’s house in the Bona Dea affair; a disciplined mother for a famously ambitious son.

characters.relation: German king in Gaul
characters.blurb: Suebian leader Caesar beat early in the Gallic War—the first taste of northern kings as Roman clients or corpses.

characters.relation: Top legate who deserted to Pompey
characters.blurb: Right hand in Gaul who chose Pompey in 49 and died in the African or Spanish wars against his old chief.

characters.relation: Young lieutenant; later consul
characters.blurb: Trusted in the civil war’s navy and politics; a Caesarian of the next generation.

characters.relation: Optimate who opposed his early offices
characters.blurb: Princeps senatus who fought Caesar’s bid for the chief pontificate and other populares moves.

characters.relation: Numidian king with the Pompeians
characters.blurb: Brought African cavalry against Caesar; defeated after Thapsus’s campaign wrecked the republican remnant.

characters.relation: Pompeian in Spain
characters.blurb: Legate beaten in the Ilerda campaign when Caesar first broke Pompey’s Spanish armies.

characters.relation: Father-in-law
characters.blurb: Calpurnia’s father; consular connection that tied Caesar to a respectable optimate house even as he broke the Republic.

characters.relation: Colleague who “watched the skies”
characters.blurb: Co-consul of 59 who tried to void Caesar’s laws by omen-watching; Romans joked the year was of Julius and Caesar.

characters.relation: Tribune bought for his cause
characters.blurb: Brilliant spendthrift who carried Caesar’s vetoes in 50 and died for him in Africa.

characters.relation: Father-in-law of his youth
characters.blurb: Marian leader whose daughter Cornelia Caesar refused to divorce at Sulla’s order.

characters.relation: Conspirator he was whispered to favor
characters.blurb: Caesar’s mildness in the Catilinarian debate let enemies smear him; Plutarch weighs the suspicion carefully.

characters.relation: Cato’s noisy follower
characters.blurb: Ape of Cato who heckled the dynasts; comic relief in the Senate’s last years.

characters.relation: Young great-nephew; heir
characters.blurb: Named in Caesar’s will as adopted son—the Octavius who becomes Augustus after the Life’s curtain.

characters.relation: Greek pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Alexander is his match: ambition measured against a world map, and a death that leaves the empire to quarreling heirs.

characters.relation: Conqueror, dictator, and subject of the Life
characters.blurb: Quaestor, pontifex maximus, consul, and dictator perpetuo—his ambition, clemency, and murder on the Ides of March made him the hinge on which the Republic turned into empire.

characters.relation: First wife; daughter of Cinna
characters.blurb: Daughter of the Marian leader Cinna; Caesar refused Sulla’s order to divorce her, risking proscription, and stayed loyal until her death left him free to marry Pompeia.

characters.relation: Optimate consul who opposed Caesar’s command extension
characters.blurb: Consul who joined Lentulus in blocking Caesar’s recall proposals; he struck a senator of New Comum to humiliate Caesar and send Pompey to war.

characters.relation: Historian and officer present at the Rubicon
characters.blurb: Caesar’s companion when he crossed the Rubicon; Pollio later recorded Caesar’s Latin words at that moment, which he himself translated into Greek in his own history.

characters.relation: Praetor before whom young Caesar pleaded
characters.blurb: Marcus Lucullus served as praetor of Macedonia when Caesar prosecuted Publius Antonius there; his namesake Lucius Lucullus appears in the Clodius scandal as Clodia’s husband.

characters.relation: Loyal agent and biographer
characters.blurb: Close supporter who managed Caesar’s business affairs; Plutarch cites Oppius’s own account of Caesar’s famous ability to dictate to several scribes simultaneously.

characters.relation: Veteran senator who joked about Caesar’s isolation
characters.blurb: Old man who told Caesar that the senators stayed away from the Forum out of fear of him; when Caesar asked why Considius himself came, the elder said age was his guard.

characters.relation: Fellow consul with Caesar; key ally
characters.blurb: Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus, Caesar’s consular colleague after the first dictatorship; he sided with Caesar against those urging peace with Pompey.

characters.relation: Historian cited on the Ides omens
characters.blurb: Titus Livius, whose account of the augur Spurinna’s prediction and of Calpurnia’s dream Plutarch mentions when describing the omens before the Ides of March.

characters.relation: Pontic king; veni, vidi, vici
characters.blurb: Son of Mithridates who overran Bithynia while Caesar was occupied in Egypt; Caesar defeated him at Zela so swiftly that he boasted ‘I came, I saw, I conquered.’

characters.relation: God whose feast-day marked Pharsalus
characters.blurb: The battle of Pharsalus was won on the feast of Bacchus—the very day Pompey had four years before set out to war. Plutarch also notes the Bona Dea rites were kept for the mother of Bacchus.

characters.relation: Gallic paramount chief
characters.blurb: Supreme Gallic commander whose father had been put to death for aiming at tyranny. He surrendered to Caesar at Alesia in full armor and was kept for the triumph.

characters.relation: King of Bithynia; host to young Caesar
characters.blurb: At his Bithynian court Caesar lingered as a youthful envoy; rumors of a romantic attachment followed Caesar for life and were revived by enemies to mock him.

characters.relation: Rhetoric teacher at Rhodes
characters.blurb: Son of Molon, famed orator at Rhodes whose pupils included Cicero; Caesar studied under him after escaping the pirates and was judged second only to Cicero in native eloquence.

characters.relation: Second heir who joined the conspiracy
characters.blurb: Named second heir in Caesar's will yet joined Cassius and Brutus in the plot; on the Ides he persuaded Caesar to ignore Calpurnia's dream and enter the Senate, then kept Antony occupied outside.

characters.relation: Conspirator who struck the first blow
characters.blurb: Gave Caesar the first cut, in the neck, with a trembling hand; Caesar seized the dagger crying “Vile Casca, what is this?”—and the rest fell on him from every side.

characters.relation: Conspirator who gave the murder signal
characters.blurb: Pretended to petition Caesar for his exiled brother; seized Caesar's robe with both hands and pulled it from his neck—the pre-arranged signal that unleashed the daggers on the Ides of March.

characters.relation: Greek teacher who tried to warn Caesar
characters.blurb: A Cnidian teacher of Greek logic who learned the conspiracy through his friendship with Brutus's circle; pressed a written warning into Caesar's hand as he approached the Senate—Caesar kept it unread.

characters.relation: Caesarian general; future triumvir
characters.blurb: Caesar's last supper host the night before the Ides; after the murder he and Antony hid in friends' houses, then emerged to take charge of Caesar's legacy and later form the second triumvirate with Octavian.

characters.relation: Intimate agent and flatterer
characters.blurb: Wealthy Spanish financier and Caesar's closest personal agent; when Caesar was about to rise for the senate's honors, Balbus stopped him—“Remember you are Caesar”—locking in the slight that inflamed the optimates.

characters.relation: Tribune who stripped the royal diadems
characters.blurb: With Marullus, tore the royal diadems from Caesar's statues and arrested the first men to salute him as king; the people called them “Brutus” in approval—Caesar dismissed them both from the tribunate.

characters.relation: Tribune who stripped the royal diadems with Flavius
characters.blurb: Acted with Flavius in removing the diadems from Caesar's statues and punishing those who hailed him king; dismissed by Caesar for the affront.

characters.relation: Poet friend of Caesar wrongly killed by the mob
characters.blurb: Caesar's friend who went to the funeral out of respect; the grieving crowd mistook him for the conspirator Cinna and tore him apart—a poet destroyed by confused popular rage.

characters.relation: Cleopatra's Sicilian confidant
characters.blurb: The one companion Cleopatra trusted with the plan; he carried her rolled in bedding through the palace gates to Caesar's quarters—Caesar was captivated before the bundle was undone.

characters.relation: Catilinarian conspirator left in Rome
characters.blurb: Gaius Cornelius Cethegus, left in Rome with Lentulus Sura when Catiline fled; Caesar pleaded in the senate for mercy, Cato pressed for death—he and Lentulus were executed on Cicero's order.

characters.relation: Pompeian legate in Further Spain
characters.blurb: Marcus Terentius Varro—scholar and polymath—who served as Pompey's governor of Further Spain; one of the two Pompeian commanders Caesar neutralized in the Ilerda campaign before crossing to Greece.

characters.relation: Roman legate killed in the Eburones ambush
characters.blurb: Lucius Aurunculeius Cotta (text spells “Costa”), co-legate with Titurius; Ambiorix lured their column into a trap and slaughtered it—the news brought Caesar racing back from Italy to exact revenge.

characters.relation: Roman legate killed in the Eburones ambush
characters.blurb: Quintus Titurius Sabinus, co-legate with Cotta; both were destroyed with their entire column by Ambiorix's ruse—one of the worst Roman reverses in the Gallic War.

characters.relation: Eburones chief who ambushed the Roman column
characters.blurb: Ambiorix of the Eburones, who feigned a friendly warning to Titurius and Cotta, then annihilated their column; his treachery ignited a general Gallic revolt and one of Caesar's most punishing winter campaigns.

characters.relation: Consul neutralized with Gallic gold
characters.blurb: Lucius Aemilius Paullus (consul 50 BC), paid fifteen hundred talents by Caesar to build the Basilica Aemilia in the Forum—a transaction that bought his silence in Caesar's last year of Gallic command.

characters.relation: Friend and agent at Rome
characters.blurb: Caesar's trusted representative in Rome; the “I came, saw, conquered” dispatch from Zela was addressed to him—yet Plutarch also names Amantius's covetousness among the embarrassments of Caesar's household.

characters.relation: Praetor killed in the soldiers' mutiny
characters.blurb: One of two praetors murdered by mutinous troops in Italy; Caesar rebuked the soldiers only by addressing them as “citizens” instead of “fellow-soldiers,” then rewarded each man with a thousand drachmas—scandalizing Rome.

characters.relation: Praetor killed alongside Cosconius
characters.blurb: Killed with Cosconius in the army mutiny; Caesar's lenient response—gentle address, generous bounty—restored order but drew censure for treating murder so lightly.

characters.relation: Caesarian officer and profligate rebuilder
characters.blurb: Came to Caesar with reinforcements before Pharsalus; later scandalized Rome by pulling down Pompey's house and rebuilding it at his own expense, not finding it grand enough—one of the men in Caesar's orbit who made him odious.

characters.relation: Officer who hunted down Pompey's elder son
characters.blurb: After Munda he tracked the elder of Pompey's two sons and brought Caesar his head—marking the final end of the republican cause in Spain.

characters.relation: Nominal Scipio placed at the head of the army
characters.blurb: A low-born man of the Scipio house whom Caesar put in nominal command of his African troops to counter the oracle that a Scipio must always conquer in Africa—whether in mockery or in earnest superstition, Plutarch cannot say.

characters.relation: Consul for a single day
characters.blurb: Appointed consul when his predecessor Maximus died with one day left in the year; Cicero quipped: “Let us make haste, lest the man be gone from office before we come.”

characters.relation: Soldier of legendary endurance at Dyrrhachium
characters.blurb: At the Dyrrhachium siege he fought on after an arrow through one eye, javelins through shoulder and thigh, and one hundred and thirty darts lodged in his shield; he feigned surrender to draw two enemies close, cut off one's arm, and drove off the other.

characters.relation: Soldier who fought one-handed at Marseilles
characters.blurb: At the sea-fight before Marseilles his sword hand was cut off; he swung his shield arm against the enemy's faces until he drove them off and took the vessel—a feat Plutarch singles out among Caesar's finest soldiers.

characters.relation: Quaestor who chose death over pardon
characters.blurb: Newly appointed quaestor captured by Scipio's fleet off Africa; Scipio offered him his life—Granius replied that Caesar's soldiers gave mercy, they did not receive it, and fell on his own sword.

characters.relation: Sulla's patrol captain whom Caesar bribed to escape
characters.blurb: The officer commanding the Sullan patrol that caught young Caesar in the Sabine hills; Caesar paid him two talents to be released—his first successful wager on his own survival.

characters.relation: Augur at Padua who foretold the Pharsalus victory
characters.blurb: An augur from Padua, acquaintance of the historian Livy; on the very day of Pharsalus he read the omens, announced the exact moment battle was joined, then leaped up crying “Caesar, you are victorious!”

characters.relation: Historian who challenged Caesar's Rhine account
characters.blurb: Tanusius Geminus, Roman annalist; gave a less flattering version of the German campaign, reporting that Cato urged Caesar be handed to the barbarians to expiate the broken truce.

characters.relation: Julia's first fiancé, displaced by Pompey
characters.blurb: Originally contracted to marry Caesar's daughter Julia; Caesar broke the match to give her to Pompey and seal the triumvirate, promising Servilius Pompey's own daughter in consolation.

characters.relation: Sulla's son; betrothal pawn of the triumvirate
characters.blurb: Faustus Cornelius Sulla, son of the dictator; Pompey's daughter had been promised to him, but the betrothal was set aside when Caesar re-arranged it to make Servilius Caepio easier to displace.

characters.relation: Philosopher who recorded the pre-Ides omens
characters.blurb: Strabo the philosopher, cited by Plutarch for the extraordinary portents before the Ides: armed men of fire contending in the sky, and a soldier's hand ablaze without harm—signs Plutarch judged worth mentioning in so great a case.

characters.relation: Fabulist for whom Caesar freed Cnidus
characters.blurb: Author of a celebrated collection of fables; to gratify him Caesar enfranchised the Cnidians on his way through Asia after Pharsalus—an act of literary patronage amid the wreckage of civil war.

characters.relation: Superintendent appointed for the Corinth canal
characters.blurb: Appointed by Caesar to supervise the cutting of the Corinth isthmus—one of the grand engineering projects Caesar planned after the wars, all left unfinished by the Ides of March.

characters.relation: Consul who died with a day left in office
characters.blurb: Quintus Fabius Maximus (consul 45 BC), who died one day before his consulship ended; Caesar filled the vacancy by appointing Caninius Revilius for that single day—and Cicero's joke about hurrying to greet him went around Rome.

characters.relation: Praetor of Asia who let the pirates escape justice
characters.blurb: Marcus Junius Juncus, praetor of Asia; when Caesar brought the captured pirates for sentencing, Junius coveted the ransom money and delayed—Caesar left him, went to Pergamus, and crucified them himself as he had promised.

characters.relation: Praetor under whom Caesar served as quaestor in Spain
characters.blurb: Gaius Antistius Vetus, praetor whom Caesar honored ever after; Caesar repaid the debt by making Vetus's son his own quaestor when the roles were later reversed.

characters.relation: Roman official prosecuted for extortion by Caesar
characters.blurb: A Roman official tried for corrupt practices before the praetor of Macedonia; Caesar argued for the Greek cities against him, but Antonius appealed to Rome's tribunes claiming he could not get fair judgment before Greeks.

characters.relation: Caesarian legate who commanded the center at Pharsalus
characters.blurb: Gnaeus Domitius Calvinus—distinct from Domitius Ahenobarbus—placed in command of Caesar's center at Pharsalus; he was later defeated by Pharnaces in Pontus, necessitating Caesar's rapid campaign that ended in the “veni, vidi, vici” dispatch.

characters.relation: Caesarian legate holding the Greek flank
characters.blurb: Quintus Fufius Calenus, one of Caesar's lieutenants in the Greek campaign; held fifteen cohorts at Megara and Athens as Caesar rallied his army before Pharsalus.

characters.relation: Host at a famous supper in Milan
characters.blurb: Entertained Caesar at table in Milan; served asparagus dressed with sweet ointment instead of oil—Caesar ate without complaint and rebuked his friends' distaste: showing disgust at another's fault proves you share it.

locations.relation: Home of his rise and fall
locations.blurb: Birthplace, forum of his ambition, and the stage of the Ides—Caesar’s story begins and ends in Rome.
As a young man he lingered at Nicomedes’ court—gossip clung to the stay, and Sylla’s enmity hardened.

locations.relation: School of rhetoric after the pirates
locations.blurb: After ransoming himself from Cilician pirates, he studied with Apollonius and later crucified his captors as promised.

locations.relation: Spanish capital of his early command
locations.blurb: Seat of his Spanish service—quaestor and later propraetor—where he first tasted provincial power and debt’s pressure.
Nearly a decade of conquest, winter camps, and dispatches—the province that made him master of an army.
He threw a timber bridge across in days, showed the standards beyond the river, then broke the span behind him.
Twice he crossed with fleets—more reconnaissance and prestige than lasting conquest, yet Rome’s first look at the island.
Double lines of works closed on Vercingetorix; the surrender in armor sealed Gaul and Caesar’s military fame.
The little stream of Italy’s boundary—once crossed with the Thirteenth, the Republic’s quarrel became open war.
He seized it at once after the crossing; Italy’s towns opened as Pompey fell back toward Brundisium.

locations.relation: Adriatic gate in the chase of Pompey
locations.blurb: Harbor of embarkation: Pompey slipped east from here; Caesar followed into Greece when ships and season allowed.
Caesar’s entrenchments nearly penned Pompey, then broke; the setback sent him inland toward Thessaly.

locations.relation: Decisive battle of the civil war
locations.blurb: On the plain of Thessaly he broke Pompey’s larger army (48 BC); the Republic’s last great field army scattered.
He found Pompey already dead, stayed for the Alexandrian war, and left Cleopatra queen—and Caesarion rumored his.
Where he crushed Pharnaces so swiftly the dispatch ran: I came, I saw, I conquered.
He shattered the republican remnant in Africa (46 BC); Cato’s suicide at Utica followed the rout.

locations.relation: Last battle of the civil wars
locations.blurb: Hardest fight of all, against Pompey’s sons in Spain (45 BC)—after Munda, no rival army remained.
The homeland he crossed with legions after the Rubicon—towns chose sides as Pompey abandoned the west.
Province of his youth’s command and of Munda’s last blood—Spain bookends his military life outside Gaul.
Where the republican cause gathered under Scipio; Thapsus decided it, and Utica heard Cato’s end.
He came hunting Pompey and stayed to settle a dynasty—Egypt’s court became a second civil war.
The Greek East of his early wanderings—Bithynia, Rhodes, and the pirate coast before Rome claimed him fully.
He followed Pompey across the Adriatic into Greek lands—Dyrrhachium first, then the plain of Pharsalia.
Inland from the Adriatic camps—the open country where Caesar chose to risk everything against Pompey.

locations.relation: Eastern shore of the civil war
locations.blurb: Named among the eastern theaters as Pompey’s allies and Caesar’s pursuit shifted across the Greek north.
Mithridates’ old realm—Pharnaces rose there until Caesar’s lightning campaign ended at Zela.
On the map of the eastern war after Egypt—Caesar’s settlements and dispatches touched Syria’s coast.
Waters of the pirates who seized him as a young man—he vowed the cross, and kept the vow.
Not Caesar’s battlefield, but the city where Cato chose death rather than live under the victor of Thapsus.
The tribes across the Rhine—Caesar’s bridge was a show of force more than a lasting occupation.
Shorelands toward Dyrrhachium—Caesar’s crossing of the Adriatic opened the Greek half of the civil war.
Named with the eastern landings as Caesar’s forces gained the Illyrian coast opposite Italy.

locations.relation: Grain island on the war’s map
locations.blurb: A hinge of supply and passage between Italy and Africa in the years of civil war.
Counted among the western possessions whose loyalty mattered when Italy itself was in arms.
The mountain screen his Gallic command looked across—winter and war moved through their passes.
The great river of southern Gaul—corridor of allies, supplies, and early campaigns.
Named in the geography of northern Italy as Caesar’s province and later his march southward.
Rome’s river—backdrop to triumphs, floods of politics, and the city’s daily life under his dictatorship.
The mouth of the Tiber—Rome’s gate to the sea in an age when grain and fleets decided wars.

locations.relation: Colony he settled in the north
locations.blurb: A Cisalpine town tied to his patronage—Plutarch notes the colony among favors that bound the north to him.

locations.relation: Old African rival on the map
locations.blurb: The ruined Punic capital still haunted Roman memory when Caesar’s African war returned Rome to those shores.

locations.relation: Greek city on the war’s horizon
locations.blurb: Named among Greek places as the civil war’s eastern chapter moved through Hellenic lands.
Another Greek landmark in the geography of Pompey’s and Caesar’s contest across the Aegean world.
